author: jtraglia
content:
Thanks again for the doc <@402080271563751435> 🙏 my opinion is pretty similar to Jihoon's. I'm still not convinced that such an attack (partition via selective gossip) is practical. In my opinion, it's only an issue _because_ of the validator blacklisting. So if we drop both resigning & the blacklist I suspect things will be okay. It doesn't feel worth it to develop a relatively complex mitigation to (1) something that might not actually be an issue and (2) definitely will not be an issue with mandatory proofs. But if we test this in a somewhat large devnet and it's shown to be an issue, my opinions will change.

> Is a peer ID tied to a specific validator key?

Your understanding is correct. You could theoretically host multiple beacon nodes connected to a single validator. But for an attacker to repeatedly connect to a victim and send them bad proofs which waste their time, they would need to operate a lot of nodes. They would need to be like 25% of the victims peers at any given time; 25 malicious peers; 25 bad proofs per slot; ~10MiB to download and ~5 seconds to verify. And since the victim would immediately reject/unpeer them, the victim will look for new peers which may or may not be run by the attacker. The network of proof validating nodes needs to be large enough that it's impractical for an attacker to perform such an attack. If there are 1000 proof validating nodes, each node has a peer limit of 100, an attacker would need to host 333 nodes in parallel with no peering limits to make up 25% of the average nodes peers.

> Yep I agree with this. Let’s wait for feedback. I will remove from the open PR for now.

Sounds good. I agree that we shouldn't let this stall progress in other areas.

author: taulepton_
content: I've taken note to consider how we can test this on a devnet. Until evidence shows otherwise we will proceed without the resigning. Your explanation adds clarity, thanks for that.
